coredata.dc:title: The synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from mango peel and their multiple applications
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Jiao, Xin-Yue
Li, Lu-shuang
Qin, Si
Zhang, Yu
Huang, Kun
Xu, Li
coredata.dc:description: Mango peel was chosen as the carbon source to generate carbon dots (CDs), for its green and potential satisfactory photoluminescent nature in addition to the waste recycling usage of mango peel. An eco-friendly approach which combined pyrolyzation with oxygenolysis was adopted to produce CDs from mango peel without any other treatment. The prepared CDs were 2–6 nm nanoparticles with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 8.5 ± 0.2%. Additionally, the CDs exhibited excellent fluorescent stability towards salt and ultraviolet irradiation, and reversible fluorescence between acid and base conditions. Owing to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence, CDs were successfully applied to cellular labeling on A549 cells in three colors (blue, green and red). Additionally, the fluorescence of CDs could be significantly quenched by Fe2+. Thus the CDs were applied as the fluorescent probe for detection of Fe2+ in ferrous succinate tablets with the linearity range of 4 μM–16 μM and the detection limit of 1.2 μM. The CDs from the mango peel were promising for multiple applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Mango peel
Carbon dots
Ferrous succinate
Cellular labeling

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs from mango peel
Measurement and calculation of fluorescence quantum yield
Detection of Fe2+ in tablet and selectivity of the analytical method
Fe2+ sensing based on fluorescence quenching of CDs

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities
National Natural Science Foundation of China

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Optimization of synthetic conditions. The effects of (a) calcination temperature of mango peel powder, (b) reaction time of carbonized ashes in concentrated H2SO4 and (c) proportion of the corresponding obtained carbonized ashes and concentrated H2SO4 on the fluorescence quantum yield of CDs.
HR-TEM images of CDs. (a) The morphology of CDs. Inset: lattice fringes of CDs. (b) Histogram of size distribution. (c) SAED image of CDs. (d) XRD pattern of CDs. (e)FTIR spectrum of CDs.
XPS spectra of CDs. (a) Survey spectrum of XPS. (b) C1 s spectrum of XPS. (c) N1 s spectrum of XPS. (d) O1 s spectrum of XPS. (e) S2p spectrum of XPS.

(a) Fluorescence excitation spectrum and emission spectrum of CDs. (b) Emission spectra of CDs with an increasing excitation wavelength from 310 nm to 400 nm. (c) Upconversion emission spectra of CDs excited from 700 nm to 800 nm. (d) The fluorescence decay curve of CDs.

The fluorescence stability of CDs under different conditions. (a) Effects of ionic strengths. (b) Fluorescence intensity of CDs under continuous excitation at 365 nm with a Xe lamp for 180 min. (c) Effects of different pHs. (d) The reversibility of fluorescence intensity in acidic and alkaline system.

Effects of pH (a), type of buffers (b) and reaction time (c) on the F/F0 in the presence of 100 μM Fe2+. (d) Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of 100 μM Fe2+ and other metal ions (PBS, pH 6.0).

(a) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in the absence and presence of different concentrations of Fe2+ at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. (b)Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Fe2+ ranging from 4 μM to 200 μM. (c)The linear relationship between F/F0 and different concentrations of Fe2+.
The images of A549 cells incubated with CDs (10 μg mL−1 for 2 h) under (a) 405 nm, (b) 488 nm and (c) 559 nm laser by laser scanning confocal microscope.
The determined content of ferrous succinate in tablets by titration and fluorimetry.

row.entry: Content of ferrous succinate (g per tablet)

head.ce:title: The synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots from mango peel and their multiple applications

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan, 430030, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Huazhong University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Tongji School of Pharmacy, Huazhong University of Science and Technology, Wuhan 430030, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Xin-Yue Jiao and Lu-shuang Li contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Mango peel was chosen as the carbon source to generate carbon dots (CDs), for its green and potential satisfactory photoluminescent nature in addition to the waste recycling usage of mango peel. An eco-friendly approach which combined pyrolyzation with oxygenolysis was adopted to produce CDs from mango peel without any other treatment. The prepared CDs were 2–6 nm nanoparticles with a high fluorescence quantum yield of 8.5 ± 0.2%. Additionally, the CDs exhibited excellent fluorescent stability towards salt and ultraviolet irradiation, and reversible fluorescence between acid and base conditions. Owing to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility and multicolor fluorescence, CDs were successfully applied to cellular labeling on A549 cells in three colors (blue, green and red). Additionally, the fluorescence of CDs could be significantly quenched by Fe2+. Thus the CDs were applied as the fluorescent probe for detection of Fe2+ in ferrous succinate tablets with the linearity range of 4 μM–16 μM and the detection limit of 1.2 μM. The CDs from the mango peel were promising for multiple applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a kind of carbonaceous materials with a size of less than 10 nm, which attracted great attention in recent years [1]. Owing to the unique photoluminescent and physicochemical properties, such as multi-color fluorescence, good solubility in aqueous and/or organic solvents, low toxicity and biocompatibility [1], CDs have found broad applications, e.g. bioimaging, biosensing, targeted drug delivery and photocatalysis, etc [2].

These fluorescent nanoparticles were initially separated from the synthetic process of single-walled carbon nanotubes by Xu et al. in 2004 [3] and named as CDs by Sun et al. in 2006 [4]. Until now, diverse organic compounds as carbon sources were adopted to generate CDs via various methods. For instance, 1,2-ethanediamine and acrylic acid produced amino functionalized CDs through microwave radiation [5]. Besides, 1,2,4-triaminobenzene, polyurethane, carbon nanopowder and hydrazine hydrate were used to synthesize N-doped CDs, respectively [6–8]. In addition, ionic liquid were used alone or together with citric acid as carbon precursor [9,10]. As a common carbon source, citric acid could be used to generate CDs via various synthetic approaches, such as microwave radiation and hydrothermal treatment [11–13]. In some cases, passivation, doping of different elements or introduction of surface functional groups was necessary to enhance the fluorescence performance of CDs [2].

As an alternative, the natural source would guarantee “green” nature of carbon source with self-passivation or self-doping and potentially excellent photoluminescent property, as it is generally nontoxic and abundant in a variety of elements. In this respect, banana juice [14], bombyx mori silk [15], tomato juice [16], mango fruit [17], konjac flour [18], rice husk [19] and Cydonia oblonga [20] were attempted as carbon sources to produce fluorescent CDs. However, the high consumption of precursors may cause a waste of available resources. Thus, domestic garbage, e.g. fruit peels [21,22], candle soot [23] and coffee grounds [24], were the potential candidate natural substances. Nevertheless, it is still a challenge to synthesize CDs with excellent physicochemical and fluorescent properties by low-cost, time-saved and relatively environmental-friendly methods.

In this study, mango peel was chosen as the carbon source, as it contains lots of functional components, such as polyphenols, carotenoids, gallic acid, flavonoids, mangiferin and rich dietary fiber [25,26], etc. To achieve the CDs with appropriate properties, the strategy, i.e. pyrolyzation at high temperature and oxygenolysis with concentrated acid, was employed to treat mango peel. Carbonization, oxidation, polymerization and nucleation [24] would be expected during the treatment. A large amount of hydroxyl, carbonyl, carboxyl and amino groups could thus be available for the obtained CDs, and the presence of N and S in the CDs indicated the occurrence of self-passivation/doping during the preparation. The quantum yield of as-prepared CDs was calculated to be 8.5 ± 0.2% in reference to quinine sulfate, which was higher than most CDs from natural source [21,22,24,27,28]. The mango peel CDs succeeded in labeling A549 cells and fluorescence sensing Fe2+ in ferrous succinate tablets. The prepared CDs were promising for multiple applications with the merits of “green” and photoluminescent properties.

The mango (Mangifera indica L.) (Australia Mango, Hainan, China) for experiment was purchased from a local fruit market (Wuhan, China). Concentrated H2SO4, NaOH, HCl, NaCl, sodium phosphate, sodium phosphate monobasic, disodium phosphate, acetone, FeSO4·7H2O, FeCl3·6H2O, CuSO4·5H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, NiSO4·6H2O, MgCl2·6H2O, Pb(NO3)2, AgNO3, ZnSO4·7H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, CdCl2·2.5H2O, MnSO4·H2O, CaCl2, Hg(CH3COO)2 and KCl were all of analytical grades and purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The ultrapure water was prepared by a Heal Fore NW system (Shanghai, China). 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) was purchased from Amresco (Ohio, USA). The human lung cancer cell line, i.e. A549 cells, was obtained from cell bank of Chinese academy of sciences (Shanghai, China).

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectrum was recorded on an AVATAR 360 transform infrared spectrometer (Thermo, USA). The UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra were recorded on a UV-3600 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan) and RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan), respectively. Morphology and size distribution of particles were observed via a JEM-2100 high resolution transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) (JEOL, Japan), and the interplanar spacing was calculated by Digital Micrograph software. The spectrum of X-ray diffraction (XRD) was recorded on an XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on an ESCALAB 250Xi spectrometer (Thermo, USA) equipped with a monochromatic Al-Kα exciting source, and the obtained spectra were deconvoluted by XPS peak software. The fluorescence lifetime was measured on a FELIX32 Fluorescence Lifetime Spectrometer (Photon Technology International, USA).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs from mango peel
ce:section.ce:para: Mango peels were washed with ultrapure water three times and then dried completely in an oven at 80 °C. The dried mango peels were ground into powder and calcinated at 300 °C under air conditions for 2 h to transform into carbonized ashes. The carbonized ashes (10 g) were immersed in concentrated H2SO4 (30 mL) at room temperature for 6 h for carbonization and oxygenolysis, and followed by the addition of ultrapure water (300 mL) and a certain amount of NaOH until neutral. The suspension was centrifuged at 4500 rpm (3622×g) for 20 min to remove black residues, and the supernatant was filtered through 0.22 μm millipore membrane. Acetone was then added into the filtrate, and some white precipitants were resulted, which were supposed to be sulfate. After centrifugation, the supernatant was added with more acetone to further precipitate sulfates. This process was repeated several times until no precipitant was resulted. The final supernatant was concentrated by evaporation, and the orange powders were obtained, which were CDs. Thus-prepared CDs were stored in the dark at ambient temperature for further use.

The preparation condition, e.g. the calcination temperature of mango peel powder, reaction time of carbonized ashes in concentrated H2SO4, and the proportion of carbonized ashes to concentrated H2SO4, were optimized based on the fluorescence quantum yield as the indicator.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement and calculation of fluorescence quantum yield
ce:section.ce:para:
The quantum yield (φ) of CDs was calculated by the Eq. (1), using quinine sulfate as a reference [29].
(1) φx=φs (Kx/Ks)(ηx/ηs)2

In the Eq. (1), subscript X and S represent CDs and quinine sulfate, respectively. φ refers to quantum yield with regard to quinine sulfate (φs = 0.54, when quinine sulfate is dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 in the case of its UV absorbance less than 0.1 at the wavelength of 360 nm). K is the slope of the curve between absorbance values at the wavelength of 360 nm as x-axis (the absorbance should be less than 0.1 in order to reduce the effect of self-absorption) and the integrated fluorescence intensities excited at 360 nm as y-axis. η denotes the refractive index of solvent. ηx/ηs equals 1 because η values of both 0.1 M H2SO4 (medium for quinine sulfate) and water (medium for CDs) are 1.33.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of Fe2+ in tablet and selectivity of the analytical method
ce:section.ce:para: Twenty microliter aqueous solution of CDs (1.5 mg mL−1) and 30 μL Fe2+ solution of respective specified concentration (i.e. 4 μM, 6 μM, 8 μM, 10 μM, 12 μM and 16 μM) were added into 2950 μL PBS (200 mM, pH 6.0). The mixture was gently shaken and reacted for 10 min at room temperature. The fluorescence spectra of the final solution were recorded at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm. For quantification, the fluorescence intensity was recorded at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm and emission wavelength of 420 nm. The selectivity of the analytical method was studied in the presence of 100 μM other metal ions, such as Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+. The quenching of fluorescence by these individual metal ions was evaluated by the ratio of the fluorescence intensity in the presence and absence of the specified metal ions (F/F0).

The developed method was used to determine the content of ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets. The ferrous succinate tablets were purchased from a local pharmacy (Wuhan, China), which was first checked to be free of Fe3+ [30,31] The pretreatment of the tablets was as below: 1 g of tablet powder was dissolved in 75 mL water and 15 mL H2SO4 (1 M) with the addition of 0.75 g zinc powder, which played a role to prevent the oxidation of Fe2+ to Fe3+. The suspension was filtered through a vertical melting funnel paved with a layer of zinc powder, and the filtrate was diluted to 2000 mL with ultrapure water [30,31]. Finally, 30 μL of the above sample solution and 20 μL of CDs solution (1.5 mg mL−1) were added into 2950 μL PBS (200 mM, pH 6.0). After a 10 min reaction, the sample solution was then subjected to the fluorescence measurement.

Eq. (2) was used to calculate the content of ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets.
(2) Ferrous succinate content (g per tablet) = 2000 × 100×C (Fe2+)×10−6×171.92 × 10-3/5
Where the “2000″ and “100″ represent the volume of the sample solution and the dilution times before fluorescence measurement; C (Fe2+) was the concentration of Fe2+ in the detection system as described above, which was directly obtained by the standard regression equation using the above measurement; “10−6” and “10−3” denote the unit conversions from “μM” to “M” and “mL” to “L”, respectively; “171.92″ is the molar mass of ferrous succinate and “5″ represents the total number of tablets for each experiment.

For comparison, the titration method [30,31] was also utilized to quantify ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets, using cerium sulfate and orthophenanthroline as titrant and indicator, respectively. The color of sample solution transformed from the orange into the green indicating the end point of titration. The experiment was performed three times in parallel and the titration results were corrected with blank test. The content of ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets using the titration method was calculated by Eq. (3).
(3) Ferrous succinate content (g per tablet) = 2000×C (Fe2+)×171.92 × 10−3/5
Where the “2000″ indicates the volume of the sample solution; C (Fe2+) was the concentration of Fe2+ determined by the titration method in the sample solution; “10−3” represents the unit conversion from “mL” to “L”; “171.92″ is the molar mass of ferrous succinate and “5″ represents the total number of tablets for each experiment.

The MTT assay was used to evaluate the cytotoxicity of as-prepared CDs on A549 cells. Briefly, cells were seeded in respective 96-well plates which were filled with DMEM medium (containing 1% penicillin/streptomycin solutions and 10% fetal bovine serum). The cells were then incubated for 24 h under the conditions of 37 °C and 5% CO2. After cells were attached to the plates, the culture medium was removed, and 100 μL fresh medium containing 10 μL CD solutions of different final concentrations (1 μg mL−1, 5 μg mL−1, 10 μg mL−1, 50 μg mL−1, 100 μg mL−1 and 500 μg mL−1) were added to each well for another 24 h incubation. Next, 10 μL MTT solution (5 mg mL−1) was added to each well and incubated for 4 h, followed by the addition of 100 μL formazen buffer to each well for another 6 h incubation to dissolve the formazan. The optical density (OD) of each well solution was measured at 490 nm with a microplate reader (Biotek, USA). The cell viability was calculated by the Eq. (4) [15].
(4) Cell viability (%)= (ODtreated/ODcontrol)×100%
Where ODtreated and ODcontrol were the OD values in the presence and absence of CDs, respectively.

A549 cells in DMEM medium were seeded in 12-well plates, respectively. Then the cells in plates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2. The aqueous solution of CDs was added to the culture medium to the final concentration of 10 μg mL−1 for another 2 h incubation. Finally, the medium was removed and 4% formaldehyde was used to immobilize the morphology of cells, followed by washing the cells with PBS. The labelled cellular images were observed by a confocal fluorescence microscopy (Olympus FV1200, Japan).

In our preliminary experiment, four strategies were tested to synthesize CDs with the dried powders of mango peel. The first one was the direct hydrothermal treatment of mango peel powder at 200 °C for 10 h, and the fluorescence quantum yield of the obtained CDs was 0.6%. The second method was the pyrolysis of powder at the temperature of 300 °C for 2 h; then the carbonized ashes were dispersed in water and subjected to an ultrasonic treatment for 15 min, and the quantum yield of the prepared CDs was 4.1%. The third method was the direct oxidation of mango peel powder by concentrated H2SO4, and the fluorescence of the as-prepared CDs was bare to see with the quantum yield of 0.2%. The fourth method by combining the second one with the third one provided highest fluorescence quantum yield, up to 8.5%. It was speculated that the large molecules were decomposed into small particles during high temperature pyrolysis, but yielding a low content of CDs; while the oxidation and self-passivation (or doping) may take place upon the treatment by the concentrated H2SO4, which possibly accounted for the enhancement of quantum yield than single pyrolysis or oxidation. Hence, the fourth strategy, i.e. pyrolysis at high temperature combined with oxidation at the room temperature (approximately 25 °C), was adopted to produce CDs from mango peel. To achieve the fluorescence as high as possible, the preparation conditions were optimized.

As shown in Fig. 1 a, pyrolysis temperature had great effects on the quantum yield of as-prepared CDs. It was speculated that the mango peel powder was pyrolyzed incompletely under low temperatures (150 °C and 200 °C), resulting in weak fluorescence. However, at the high temperature above 400 °C, the organics in the powder were prone to burn off as a result of the high degree of charring [32]; thus, the content of N and S elements was decreased accompanying with the decreasing CD yield. Hence, 300 °C was compromised as the optimal calcination temperature in terms of high quantum yield and CD product yield. The oxidation (or carbonation) duration of carbonized ashes in concentrated H2SO4 was then investigated from 4 h to 14 h, as seen in Fig. 1b. Fluorescence quantum yield reached a maximum at 6 h. Finally, as displayed in Fig. 1c, quantum yield of CDs was less influenced by the ratios of carbonized ashes to concentrated H2SO4, possibly because all the studied ratios ensured the complete carbonization. For the purpose of environmental protection and reagent saving, the proportion of 1 g ashes: 3 mL concentrated H2SO4 was applied in the preparation process.

To be noted, the CDs obtained through this strategy without any extra passivation and dialysis had uniform particle size and excellent fluorescence as discussed below. The fluorescence quantum yield of CDs prepared at the optimized condition was calculated to be 8.5 ± 0.2% at the excitation wavelength of 360 nm. Although the mango fruit was once used to produce CDs of different colors, the fluorescence quantum yields ranged from 0.48% to 3.92% [17]. In addition, compared to other natural sources, e.g. coffee grounds (3.8%) [24], soy milk (2.6%) [27], grass (4.2%) [28], pomelo peel (6.9%) [21] and watermelon peel (7.1%) [22], the quantum yield of mango peel CDs herein was higher. This may be related to the nature of mango peel, which contains N, S elements and lots of functional components, resulting in the CDs with satisfactory fluorescence [33]. Moreover, as discussed above, the synthetic strategy may be another attribution for the high fluorescence quantum yield. Carbonization, oxidation, polymerization and nucleation [24] during pyrolyzation at high temperature and oxygenolysis with concentrated acid could take place. Anyway, the adopted preparation method was simple, time-saving and effortless.

The CDs were well dispersed in aqueous solution. Fig. 2 a shows the HR-TEM images of CDs, and they were quasi spherical. The size distribution of CDs was uniform with the diameter from 2 nm to 6 nm with an average of 3 nm, as counted in Fig. 2b. Lattice fringes of CDs could be observed in the HR-TEM image with a lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, reflecting the in-plane lattice distance of graphite, and the distance of 0.42 nm, which was larger than the inter-layer distance of graphite (0.34 nm) [34], possibly due to the modification of oxygen-containing groups and the introduction of heteroatoms of N and S [14,18,34]. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in Fig. 2c and the XRD spectrum in Fig. 2d also disclose the presence of the diffraction planes (002) in CDs, which was in accordance with graphitic carbon [14,18].

Fig. 2(e) displays the FT-IR spectrum of CDs. The characteristic peak at 3431 cm−1 was considered to be generated from stretching vibrations of OH and/or NH, and the peak at 1636 cm−1 was identified as the carbonyl group. The absorption peaks at 2960 cm−1 and 1385 cm−1 were attributed to CH and CN bonds, respectively. Additionally, the strong peak at 1112 cm−1 was possibly ascribed to CO, CS and/or CN bond. From above analysis, the prepared CDs probably contained elements of S and N in addition to C and O, which were of great benefit to improve fluorescence quantum yield [33]. Furthermore, plenty of hydrophilic groups such as carboxyl, carbonyl, hydroxyl and amino groups may exist on the surface of CDs, which was the explanation to their favorable compatibility with aqueous medium.

The XPS spectrum is exhibited in Fig. 3 a. The peaks at the binding energy of 285 eV, 533 eV, 401 eV and 169 eV corresponded to C 1s, O 1s, N 1s and S 2p, respectively. The element analysis from XPS peak reveals that the CDs contained C, O, N and S at the percentage of 34.4%, 62.48%, 1.34% and 1.78% in this order. The high-resolution spectra were generated in Fig. 3b–e. In Fig. 3b, five peaks at the binding energy of 288.8 eV, 286.9 eV, 286.3 eV, 285.2 eV and 284.6 eV were regarded as CO/C = N, CO, CN, CS and CC/CC bonds, respectively [33]. Moreover, the N 1s spectrum, as depicted in Fig. 3c, was composed of two peaks at 400.5 eV and 399.8 eV, which were attributed to NH- andN- bonds, respectively [35]. In Fig. 3d, COH/COC and CO bonds could be expected in CDs because of the appearance of the peaks at 532.9 eV and 532.2 eV [36], respectively. The two primary peaks in S 2p spectrum (Fig. 3e) which situated at 169.4 eV and 168.4 eV were identified as CSOx(x = 2, 3, 4) and SO bonds, respectively [33]. All of the functional groups speculated above mirrored the results of the FT-IR analysis, and the introduction of heteroatoms would have a positive effect on the fluorescence properties of CDs.

There was no obvious absorption peak for CDs in UV spectrum (spectrum not shown), which was identical with the previous study [23]. This phenomenon may be ascribed to the partially similar properties between CDs and indirect bandgap semiconductor nanomaterials [23]. Additionally, the aqueous solution of CDs was transparent and yellow-colored under the natural light; however, it emitted strong blue fluorescence under the UV light of 360 nm.

The fluorescence spectra of CDs are shown in Fig. 4 a–c. As displayed in Fig. 4a, the maximum excitation wavelength and emission wavelength are located at 310 nm and 425 nm, respectively. As the excitation wavelength increased, the emission wavelength had a red shift accompanying the decrease of fluorescence intensity to some extent, as shown in Fig. 4b. This excitation-dependent emission behavior of CDs was generally observed [14,24], possibly due to the different particle sizes and surface emissive traps with some unclear reasons. Moreover, upconversion emission spectra of CDs were observed in Fig. 4c under excited wavelengths from 700 nm to 800 nm, which was similar to the previous report [36]. The upconversion CDs were considered to have the excellent capability of near-infrared light penetration, weak autofluorescence and little damage to the normal tissues, which were promising for biological analysis, photodynamic therapy [37] and bio-imaging of cells or tissues [36]. Fig. 4d exhibited the fluorescence decay curve of CDs, and the lifetime was determined to be 3.24 ± 0.18 ns.

The fluorescence stability of as-prepared CDs under different pH systems, different concentrations of NaCl and UV irradiation duration were also studied. As shown in Fig. 5 a, the fluorescence intensity remained almost unchanged in the presence of NaCl of 0–1.0 mol L−1, which demonstrates that the ionic strength had no obvious effect on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs. Moreover, CD aqueous solution had a good anti-photobleaching ability, since their fluorescence intensity was not significantly changed under continuous excitation at 365 nm with a Xe lamp for 180 min, as reflected in Fig. 5b. Nevertheless, the solution pH exhibited a relatively obvious influence on the fluorescence intensity of the CDs, as depicted in Fig. 5c. As discussed above, CDs were speculated to have plenty of amino and carboxyl groups on their surface, which may make the particles be amphoteric, like amino acid. Thus, the prepared CDs may be charged corresponding to the surrounding pH values, resulting in the varied fluorescence intensity [35]. Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 5d, the fluorescence intensity could be recovered by switching the pH between the acidic (pH 2.0) and alkaline (pH 12.0) conditions; for repeated 8 cycles, the change of the fluorescence with the pH was consistent. This reflected the excellent fluorescence recovery ability of CDs to the environmental pHs, implying the feasible applicability of CDs in different pHs. The reasonable explanation for this observation may be the protonation and deprotonation of = N- atoms on the surface of CDs under acidic and alkaline conditions [18], which was reversible.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fe2+ sensing based on fluorescence quenching of CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence of the prepared CDs was found to be quenched by Fe2+, which may be due to the interaction of Fe2+ with the carboxyl, hydroxyl and amino groups on the surface of the CDs, leading to the agglomeration of the particles and thus the weakened fluorescence [38]. Hence, the mango peel CDs were attempted to the detection of Fe2+.

In order to improve the sensitivity, the pH value, the type of the buffer solution and reaction time for the detection system were then studied in detail. In Fig. 6 a, the F/F0 reached the minimum at pH 6.0, indicating that this pH was the optimal for sensitivity. Additionally, four kinds of buffers, including sodium acetate-acetic acid (NaAcO-HAcO) buffer, Britton-Robinson (BR) buffer, Tris−HCl buffer and PBS, were tested. As shown in Fig. 6b, the fluorescence of CDs could be quenched by Fe2 + to the greatest extent in PBS, while almost no significant quench could be observed in other three studied buffers. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 6c, the reaction time between CDs and Fe2+ from 10 min to 60 min exerted no significant influence on F/F0. Considering the time effectiveness of the assay, 10 min was a reasonable choice.

The selectivity of the method was investigated in the presence of other metal ions (Fe3+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Ni2+, Mg2+, Pb2+, Ag+, Zn2+, Al3+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, K+ and Na+) under the same conditions as the detection of Fe2+. The results in Fig. 6d illustrated that Fe2+ quenched the fluorescence of CDs distinctly, followed by Fe3+. The developed method aimed to quantify Fe2+ in ferrous succinate tablets. As no Fe3+ was expected in ferrous succinate tablets and zinc powder was used in the pretreatment process of tablets (as described in section 2.5), Fe3+ should have no interference to the determination of Fe2+ in this case. In addition, other studied metal ions exerted little or no influence on the quenching of fluorescence.

Fig. 7 a presents the fluorescence spectra of CDs coexisted with different concentrations of Fe2+ (i.e. 0 μM, 4 μM, 8 μM, 10 μM, 20 μM, 50 μM, 100 μM and 200 μM) at the excitation wavelength of 310 nm, indicating that the fluorescence intensity of CDs decreased with the increasing concentration of Fe2+, and a slight redshift of the maximum emission wavelength was observed correspondently. Fluorescence quenching of CDs in the presence of Fe2+ ranging from 4 μM to 200 μM is displayed in Fig. 7b. It was not a conventional linear relationship between the F/F0 and the concentration of Fe2+, and thus both static and dynamic quenching mechanism were speculated to be responsible for this quenching process [28]. However, it turned out that there was a good linear relationship between F/F0 and the concentration of Fe2+ in the range of 4–16 μM with a detection limit of 1.2 μM, which was suitable to determine the content of succinate ferrous in ferrous succinate tablets. As shown in Fig. 7c, the regression equation was fitted to be F/F0=−0.0081C(Fe2+)+1.0244, and the correlation coefficient was 0.9918. As listed in Table 1 , the content of ferrous succinate calculated by Eq. (2) was almost the same as that measured by the titration method and consistent with the labelled amount of ferrous succinate in the tablets (0.1 g per tablet), demonstrating the developed Fe2+ sensor based on mango peel CDs was quick and reliable, and was suitable for the determination of ferrous succinate in ferrous succinate tablets.

MTT assay was performed to investigate the cytotoxicity. The cell viability was still more than 90% even at the high concentration of 500 μg mL−1 after 24 h incubation with CDs (Fig. S1). This result fully demonstrates that the prepared CDs were almost non-toxic to A549 cells and thus the good biocompatibility of CDs. Hence, the applicability of CDs in bio-system could be highly expected.

Herein, celluar labeling by the prepared CDs was attempted. As photographed in Fig. 8 , three different colors (blue, green and red) of fluorescence when excited at wavelengths of 405 nm, 488 nm and 559 nm, respectively, could be found in A549 cells. Owing to the small particle size and water-solubility of CDs, the intracellular CDs mainly distributed in the cytoplasm. The mechanism of cellular uptake process was presumed to be endocytosis [24]. The data of both cytotoxicity test and cellular labeling indicate the applicability potential of the prepared mango peel CDs to biomedicine instead of traditional dyes.

The mango peels were successful to generate carbon dots (CDs) by pyrolysis at high temperature combined with oxidation at the room temperature. The CDs exhibit relatively high fluorescence quantum yield and good chemical inertness and photostability. Fe2+ was precisely quantified by the fluorescence quenching of CDs and this fluorescent sensor was then applied to the detection of ferrous succinate in the tablets with good accuracy. In addition, due to the low toxicity and small particle size, CDs were easily uptaken by A549 cells, implying some potential in the field of biological imaging. In conclusion, the CDs prepared with mango peels showed potential for multiple applications.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial support of this research by the Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 31471208 & 31671195) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (No. 2017TS135). The authors also thank the Analytical and Testing Center of HUST for instrumental support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfa.2019.05.073.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
